medications.drug_name: Cerumenolytic ear drops (docusate sodium 0.5% or olive oil)
medications.indication_rationale: Softening drops are the correct first step for a confirmed cerumen impaction — they soften the plug over several days so it can be removed safely by micro-suction or syringing. In telehealth this should be paired with, not substituted for, a prompt in-person examination, because a sudden unilateral hearing loss cannot be attributed to wax without otoscopy.
medications.dose_route_frequency: Docusate sodium 0.5% (or olive oil) ear drops instilled into the right ear twice daily for 5 days
contraindications_in_this_case: Avoid if there is any suspicion of tympanic-membrane perforation until the eardrum has been confirmed intact — the drum cannot currently be visualised.

medications.indication_rationale: Reasonable for symptomatic relief of the pressure otalgia while awaiting examination and wax removal. No specific dose is authored here beyond standard over-the-counter labelling.

There is no evidence of an infective otitis externa or media (afebrile, no discharge), so topical antibiotics are not indicated; several are potentially ototoxic if the eardrum is perforated, which has not been excluded. Prescribing them here would be an over-treatment error of commission.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: After instilling the softening drops, lie on the opposite (left) side for about 5 minutes so the drops pool against the wax plug and penetrate rather than running straight out.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Positional dwell time improves cerumenolytic penetration of the deep, dry plug.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Do not insert cotton buds, bobby pins or fingers into the ear canal.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Instrumentation pushes wax deeper against the eardrum, worsens impaction and risks perforation.

patient_education_points.point: Never insert cotton buds, bobby pins or fingers into the ear canal — it pushes wax deeper and can damage the eardrum.

The eardrum can't be fully seen while the wax is blocking the canal, so a small perforation can't be completely ruled out until the wax is cleared — which is why an in-person ear examination is needed.
Hearing may stay muffled and can temporarily feel worse as the drops turn the hard plug into a soft slurry; it usually only fully clears once the softened wax is removed by a clinician.
The likely explanation is that dry wax absorbed water in the shower, swelled and blocked the canal — the drops soften it over a few days so it can be safely removed.

follow_up_plan.interval: Same-day in-person ear examination for the sudden hearing loss; then GP review in 5–7 days for micro-suction or syringing once the wax is softened
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Otoscopic visualisation and integrity of the tympanic membrane once the wax is cleared, and recovery of hearing; if hearing does not recover after wax removal, arrange urgent audiology/ENT review to exclude a sensorineural component.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Sudden severe room-spinning vertigo, facial weakness, bleeding or discharge from the ear, high fever, or severe/worsening pain behind the ear — seek urgent/emergency care.

must_include_items: Recognise that a sudden unilateral hearing loss cannot be examined over chat and requires prompt in-person otoscopy and tuning-fork testing.
Arrange a same-day in-person ear examination to visualise the canal/eardrum and exclude sudden sensorineural hearing loss and tympanic-membrane perforation.
Safety-net explicitly: seek emergency care for true vertigo, facial weakness, bleeding/discharge from the ear, or high fever with pain behind the ear.
Advise against inserting cotton buds or objects into the ear canal.
acceptable_alternatives: Recommending cerumenolytic softening drops (olive oil or docusate) is appropriate ONCE cerumen is the confirmed/most likely cause, provided a prompt in-person examination is still arranged.
Simple analgesia (e.g. paracetamol) for the pain while awaiting examination.
errors_of_omission: Failing to arrange in-person examination for the sudden hearing loss.
Failing to safety-net for vertigo, facial weakness, or ear bleeding/discharge.
errors_of_commission: Confidently diagnosing 'just ear wax' and giving only self-care with no in-person examination — anchoring that could miss sudden sensorineural hearing loss.
Recommending ear syringing/irrigation on a hard, complete impaction or while eardrum integrity is unknown (perforation risk).
Recommending cotton-bud or manual removal of the wax.
Over-triaging benign wax to an ambulance/000 when no red flag is present.
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass requires arranging prompt in-person examination for the sudden hearing loss (which cannot be excluded remotely) AND explicit safety-netting. Softening-drops-only with no examination is an under-triage failure; ear-syringing advice on this impaction is an error of commission.
